examples.title: Finite Automaton for Binary Strings Divisible by 3
examples.description: Implements a DFA that accepts binary strings representing numbers divisible by 3
examples.explanation: This Python code implements a deterministic finite automaton that accepts binary strings representing numbers divisible by 3. States represent remainders (0, 1, 2) when dividing by 3. The transition function updates the remainder based on the next binary digit.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of input string
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - constant space regardless of input size

examples.title: Recursive Descent Parser for Arithmetic Expressions
examples.description: Implements a simple parser using recursive descent for expressions with +,-,*,/
examples.explanation: This recursive descent parser implements a context-free grammar for arithmetic expressions. It follows the operator precedence: factors (highest), then terms, then expressions (lowest). The parser builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) representing the structure.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion depth and AST storage

examples.title: Context-Free Grammar Definition for Simple Language
examples.description: Defines CFG for a simple programming language with variables and basic operations
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates a context-free grammar definition for a simple programming language. It shows how grammars specify the syntax rules: PROGRAM consists of statements, EXPR handles addition/subtraction, TERM handles multiplication/division, and FACTOR handles parentheses and atomic values. This CFG enforces operator precedence through its structure.

examples.title: General Recursive Descent Parser Algorithm
examples.description: General algorithm for recursive descent parsing showing the parser structure
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RecursiveDescentParser
// Grammar: E → T { (+|-) T }
//         T → F { (*|/) F }
//         F → (E) | id | number

FUNCTION Parse()
BEGIN
    RETURN ParseExpr()
END

FUNCTION ParseExpr()
BEGIN
    node ← ParseTerm()
    WHILE CurrentToken = '+' OR CurrentToken = '-' DO
        op ← CurrentToken
        Advance()
        right ← ParseTerm()
        node ← CreateNode(op, node, right)
    ENDWHILE
    RETURN node
END

FUNCTION ParseTerm()
BEGIN
    node ← ParseFactor()
    WHILE CurrentToken = '*' OR CurrentToken = '/' DO
        op ← CurrentToken
        Advance()
        right ← ParseFactor()
        node ← CreateNode(op, node, right)
    ENDWHILE
    RETURN node
END

FUNCTION ParseFactor()
BEGIN
    IF CurrentToken = '(' THEN
        Advance()
        node ← ParseExpr()
        IF CurrentToken ≠ ')' THEN
            ERROR "Expected ')''"
        ENDIF
        Advance()
    ELSE IF CurrentToken = id OR CurrentToken = number THEN
        node ← CreateLeaf(CurrentToken)
        Advance()
    ELSE
        ERROR "Unexpected token"
    ENDIF
    RETURN node
END

FUNCTION CreateNode(op, left, right)
BEGIN
    node.type ← 'OPERATOR'
    node.operator ← op
    node.left ← left
    node.right ← right
    RETURN node
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the general structure of a recursive descent parser. Each non-terminal in the grammar has its own function. The parser uses lookahead to decide which production to apply and builds an AST by recursively calling functions. This top-down parsing approach follows the grammar structure directly.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of tokens in the input
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the parse tree (recursion depth)
